Talk to me a little bit about your time at AirDNA and then specifically like a client that you had and just or one that you can most recall and how your strategy process worked end to end from like who this client was, like how did you do your research to the strategy you developed and then like what you were tracking as like goals or outcomes.
For the strategy.
And then, yeah, that's the first piece and the last one after.
Okay, sure.
Yeah, I guess I think it makes the most sense for me to just talk to AirDNA's content strategy and how it's evolved.
Thinking of AirDNA as a client, when I first started with them, they had very low output, irregular blog articles.
So the initial strategy was to create a lot of content, like pretty high volume with.
Like, a lot of keyword coverage just to build authority kind of across industry keywords.
And then about a year in and 200 blog articles later, we kind of was running out of high-quality keyword opportunities because we had built up such great coverage.
And so it was time to pivot the strategy.
We had kind of, like, milked the quick wins as much as we could.
So I did, like, a performance deep dive into our blog library, what's working, like, what's really moving the needle.
From us, for metrics, not just traffic, but quality of the traffic, looking at registrations, conversions, pretty much, and conversion rate.
And what I found when I did that was that there was maybe a dozen articles that were driving 80% of product conversion.
So the next question was, okay, like, what do those have in common, right?
In the case of AirDNA, was product tutorials, it was unique data insights, and then, like, a middle, bottom-of-funnel keyword focus.
So pretty much the type of content that speaks to folks who are more interested in converting.

So from there, I built a strategy that pretty much halved our total blog output and instead focused on both net new but also optimizations of a much smaller quantity of articles that took more time and energy to produce, but at the end of the day, created more conversions.
So that was the strategy that we ended up executing and it really paid off.
Like within three months, we had essentially doubled the number of blog articles that were successfully converting and we were doing it with half the output.
So I feel like that's like the best story for myself that I can think of that's like a work smarter, not harder outcome.
Yeah.
Does that answer your question?
I'm trying to think, yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, what was the most challenging?
Yeah.
A strategy like that.
like, hey, we're going to do something new.
And then to be able to make a pitch for it that's based on data and then also deliver on that is really rewarding.
Yeah, totally.
How are you thinking about AEO?
And there's SEO and now there's a clear behavior shift in the way people are searching for information and what they're going to, right?
And so like the way we think about it is we have a few different audiences now.
It's like who the actual person we're speaking to is, right?
But then there's these LLMs that are coming to our website and then there's bot traffic, which like underlies all of that.
But curious as to what you've seen and how you're thinking about AEO.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I think AEO is a little bit tricky, partly because I think tracking it, there's, I don't feel like we've, I've found a platform necessarily.
That does a great job of tracking it.

And especially like, partly because a lot of the AI tracking platforms, like citation platforms that are out there, it's like you don't necessarily understand like what prompts they're basing, like they're tracking your performance on, or you have to provide your own prompts to be tracking.
And that provide, that requires like a level of understanding of how people are using these tools that I think is still forthcoming because it's such a new tool.

But that aside, I, I, AEO has not necessarily influenced my approach to content in the sense that like high quality content that has like unique data insights or unique perspective, like those things that are going to perform well from an SEO perspective are also going to perform well from an AEO perspective.
Most of my work, like that takes into consideration AEO is how that content is structured, right?
So making sure that it's like written in a way that it can be easily crawled.
And, you know, is optimized for that snippet preference that LLMs have.
And then also, like, technical SEO work.
So making sure that every blog article has schema and kind of has those back-end infrastructure in place to make sure that it's accessible to LLMs crawling.
Totally.
Yeah, that makes sense.
We didn't think there was an amazing tool.
Yeah, well, there's two things.
There's one is, like, the tracking, right, which is kind of, like, a race to the bottom because there's so many companies now that track AI visibility.
And so we didn't think any were doing a great job.
We actually – I just sent it in the chat.
We just launched our own AI visibility tool.
And basically, like, what our product will do soon is connect all the dots, right, because you're not just tracking your AI visibility.
Like, what matters is how you're using that to inform a content strategy or your approach or, like, drive impact in the areas that matter.
And so we're pulling that.
In to our product with all of, you know, the traditional SEO insights and how the content we've already produced is doing, etc.
And like creating this like operating system, plus technical setup, page structure, every page on your site, etc.
So it's like a technical audit, an AEO audit, and an SEO audit tool all in one, pretty much plus the content workflow layer as well.
And so, wow.
Yeah, it's pretty insane.
Yeah, that's a lot.
That's, that covers a lot for sure.
Yeah.

And so like, he's who we are building it for.
And then like, we evolve the product based on what we hear from customers, like what actually is working with Yeah.

And that's a super hard process.
So, yeah, the short answer is like it blends everything together.

guess what typically most recently, like when you were working with William at AirDNA, like how are you reporting progress?
Like what was your typical like cadence like?
What were you focused on measuring?
Like help me understand what that

I would say primarily on a monthly basis was when I was doing the most comprehensive reporting, doing month-over-month comparisons, looking at keyword, keyword growth, also keyword rankings, right, top three, top ten, and AI citations as well as part of that.

And then engaged sessions, I would say, like, the three most important metrics for AirDNA and in general for content, would say, like, engaged sessions for traffic, conversions and conversion rate were the three north stars.
But also looking at things like engagement rate, bounce rate as well, technical health, doing that on a monthly basis, always looking at top performers.
So, like, top performing overall, month-over-month growers.
So, like, looking, okay, what content that we've produced recently is successful and why.
Also, the biggest losers.
Always kind of a fun one.
Like, okay, what's dying here and why?
And like, can we address that?
Is it worth saving?
Especially for a client like AirDNA that has like over 500 blog articles, it can be hard to optimize all of them on a regular cadence.
So there's some like prioritization that needs to happen there that the data analysis helps address.
So I'd say monthly on a quarterly basis.
And then a new type of report that I've started rolling out recently is like a quarterly audit of our different content types.
So measuring the different impact kind of within clusters, right?
Because you have blog content.
AirDNA is a data analytics company.
So we also have research reports.
We have programmatic pages where, you know, we pull in parts of the app, make it ungated and publicly accessible and SEO driven.
So we have these kind of different content buckets.
They all take different amounts.

So wanting to make sure that the resources are being allocated for like maximum impact of being like, okay, we know it costs 800 bucks to produce this type of content, 300 bucks to produce this type of content.
Here's the ROI on it.
So that way we can kind of regularly reassess like what type of content is effective.
Yeah, that makes sense.
It's pretty comprehensive.
Yeah, it's just important because, you know, our clients come to us to be like, to have that expertise to be that thought partner and like leader in the engagement.
They're coming us again to us for expertise.
And so like, part of that is always knowing like where we're going, where we're driving the boat, how to be constantly proactive.
And you can only really know that once you're seeing like the early signals and like how you're responding to them.
So that was the context behind asking that question.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Quick follow up on that is, so when we're, when we're onboarding a client, like it, does it always begin with an audit?
But to understand like what their current...

actually haven't heard a squeak.

The short answer is when we onboard a new client, we take them through an eight-week strategy sprint where we go in and uncover all the opportunities that we can do for them.
In terms of what's the best way to ingest all their context and store it so that we can create high-quality, scalable content.
Let's get a calibration article in front of them to go back and forth to download all that feedback from them.
And as part of that, it's connecting to their systems.
So like their analytics and tracking systems, GSC, GA4, anything else they're doing, the track conversions, and their website overall.
Because it's table stakes to have perfectly sound technical SEO for the work that...
We do, right?

To get a shot on goal, to even think about conversion, you need an article to be published, then that article to get indexed very quickly, and then that article that's indexed to start getting impressions, and then down to clicks and conversions later on.
So we get ahead of that right at the beginning of the process.

It's not like in every contract anymore that we will deliver a technical site audit, but if there's anything, an example like a client that I'm helping right now, they have a pretty brand new website with a couple blogs, and so I know they haven't thought through the fundamentals of technical SEO in a way that we probably would need, and so we're doing one for them just to get ahead of it, because I don't want to be hit in week six when we're trying to publish that, oh wow, we have to go fix all of these things, because they didn't exist before.
So yeah, the long answer is yes, we do.
Some of it is less formal.
Then like an actual check the box deliverable though.
Okay.
Gotcha.
But our tool, again, our platform will do all that as well.
Okay.
Gotcha.
Yeah, that makes sense.
I just, it's interesting thinking through, because you're going to have clients that are coming from so many different places, right?
Some that have like a really established content footprint, really strong technical help.
And then some people who are just starting out.
So it's like, how do you kind of balance like a standardized package of services versus like customizing it to like each client's need?
Yeah.
Yeah.

it's actually, I mean, ideally.

love to hear like what excites you about that.

Processes and, like, that accounts need to hit to be more likely to succeed.

Do they have experience in marketing?
Are they truly trusting in us having control?

of the engagement and like driving and in our strategy, like are they bought into our strategy and what entrusting our process of this is going to take a bit, we're going to get to early signals in the first eight weeks, we're going to fine tune this engine to be set up for long-term success and scale.
But conversions like that, that will take time, but it will come.
So trust us basically.
And do we have someone who trusts us?
Layers of review and like complexity of the content.

If they're, if we have someone who trusts us, they're usually the one person who reviews or we're very close with the actual other person that reviews where we've run into issues in the past is where there's like three different levels of reviews to our subject matter experts and like one half of their job is what we're doing and they don't want us to like come in and replace their job.
And so, um, that was when we were becoming more word babysitters and like getting stuck in these layers of review and, um, far too in the weeds.
